content:
# The Engine at Rest

Martha found the old locomotive at the edge of the salvage yard on a Tuesday, half-swallowed by blackberry canes. She pressed her palm against its flank and felt the cold iron hold its silence the way iron always does — without apology.

She came back the next day with a wire brush. And the day after that with primer.

The foreman watched her from the office window, coffee going lukewarm in his hand. "That thing hasn't moved in thirty years," he called out once, leaning against the doorframe. "Boiler's cracked. Drive rods are seized solid."

Martha looked up. A smear of rust-red paint crossed her cheekbone like a scar she'd earned. "I know," she said.

He stopped mentioning it after that.

She worked in the early mornings before the yard grew loud, when the light came in low and amber and turned the dew on the grass into something almost precious. She learned the engine's proportions the way you learn a face — slowly, by returning to it. The curve of the smoke deflector. The particular weight of a coupling pin. The way the cab smelled of old grease and rain.

By October, the blackberry canes were gone. The iron gleamed dully in the autumn light.

The engine still didn't move. Martha set down her brush, stood back, and looked at what she had done.

Some things, she understood, are worth tending regardless.
